Duplicate DHIS2 Indicators, Divergent Results

A Malaria RDT Test Positivity Rate Case Study from Nigeria

Published

May 30, 2026

Purpose: Worked example for the DHIS2 Reporting Checklist — Domain 1 (Data Dictionary). Demonstrates why reporting the specific DHIS2 indicator UID, and verifying its formula against instance metadata, is essential. Data: Nigeria national HMIS (DHIS2), Kebbi State, 2020–2026.

1 Background

DHIS2 instances at national scale accumulate indicators over time as programmes, donors, and implementing partners add metrics without auditing what already exists. When two indicators carry similar names and appear to measure the same construct, analysts may select either one without realising the formulas differ. The problem is compounded by the fact that DHIS2 indicator names are assigned by the instance administrator and carry no enforced schema. A name that includes the acronym “TPR” does not guarantee the formula is correctly specified.

Nigeria’s national Health Management Information System (NHMIS), deployed on DHIS2, contains at least two pre-computed indicators that nominally measure RDT test positivity rate (TPR). Their formulas differ substantially. Indicator B (uiA07P72s2Z, named “Test Positivity Rate(TPR) (RDT)”) contains a formula error that adds the RDT positive count to the denominator. Indicator A (l2eIpdluuTI, named “% of Fever cases tested positive with RDT”) uses the default aggregation of each data element — the approach consistent with the intended formula. Indicator A is not without its own data quality concern: its denominator data element, “Persons presenting with fever & tested by RDT,” should capture only those who actually received an RDT, but if health workers record total fever attendees rather than exclusively tested patients, the denominator is inflated — deflating TPR below the true value.

This document:

  1. Describes the two indicators and their exact data element and category option combo compositions, derived directly from DHIS2 instance metadata.
  2. Shows the empirical divergence between the two indicators using state-level data from Kebbi State (January 2020 – April 2026), including a pre-transition period where the Indicator B would report ~100% TPR (not visible in available data because DHIS2 suppresses values exceeding 100%).
  3. Documents that published studies and programme reports cite Nigeria DHIS2 TPR values without specifying which indicator was used.

2 Two DHIS2 indicators for RDT test positivity rate

Nigeria’s DHIS2 instance contains the following two indicators:

Indicator name UID Numerator Denominator Issues
% of Fever cases tested positive with RDT l2eIpdluuTI
(Indicator A)		 Persons tested positive for malaria by RDT (GEd2F6skCpT)
default — all age groups summed		 Persons presenting with fever & tested by RDT (r6WOvUlcQm6)
default — all age groups summed		 Denominator may include untested fever cases → TPR deflated
Test Positivity Rate(TPR) (RDT) uiA07P72s2Z
(Indicator B)		 Persons tested positive for malaria by RDT (GEd2F6skCpT):
<5yrs old (W50DgZrPrUk)
>=5years old (ItGQGDRFD4t)
≥5yrs excl PW new (v0QZcUTuyjD)
Preg Women new (V86mzY788hR)
[missing: <5yrs new (Bl7e1tsRGoZ)]		 GEd2F6skCpT default — ERROR (adds all positives)
Persons presenting with fever & tested by RDT (r6WOvUlcQm6):
<5yrs new (Bl7e1tsRGoZ)
≥5yrs excl PW new (v0QZcUTuyjD)
Preg Women new (V86mzY788hR)
[missing: old-scheme <5yrs + >=5years]		 Positives added to denominator; COC scheme mix; missing new <5yrs positives

Category option combo (COC) UID reference:

COC UID Age group Scheme (category combination)
W50DgZrPrUk <5yrs Old — Age(Malaria)
ItGQGDRFD4t >=5years Old — Age(Malaria)
Bl7e1tsRGoZ <5yrs New — NH19_Malaria Testing
v0QZcUTuyjD ≥5yrs (excl PW) New — NH19_Malaria Testing
V86mzY788hR Preg Women (PW) New — NH19_Malaria Testing

Indicator B’s name — “Test Positivity Rate(TPR) (RDT)” — contains the “TPR” acronym that programme staff and analysts routinely search for. Indicator A’s name uses different terminology (“fever cases tested positive”) and will not appear in a search for “TPR.” An analyst opening the Nigeria DHIS2 analytics interface and filtering indicators by “TPR” would find Indicator B first.

2.1a Denominator concern for Indicator A

Indicator A’s denominator data element, “Persons presenting with fever & tested by RDT” (r6WOvUlcQm6), is designed to count persons who both presented with fever and received an RDT — making it the intended denominator for HMIS-based TPR. However, if health workers record total fever attendees in this field regardless of whether they were tested (a documented data quality risk in high-workload facilities with incomplete RDT access), the denominator would exceed the actual number tested. Since persons not tested cannot be positive, the numerator remains unchanged while the denominator grows, deflating the reported TPR below the true positivity rate among those tested. This is distinct from Indicator B’s formula error, but the direction of bias is the same: both indicators can understate true TPR, through different mechanisms.

2.1 Data elements

Both indicators reference the same two data elements:

Data Element UID Description
Persons tested positive for malaria by RDT GEd2F6skCpT Numerator component
Persons presenting with fever & tested by RDT r6WOvUlcQm6 Denominator component

Both data elements belong to the category combination NH19_Malaria Testing, which in the current (post-2020) NHMIS form has three category option combos:

Category COC UID
<5yrs Bl7e1tsRGoZ
≥5yrs (excl PW) v0QZcUTuyjD
Preg Women (PW) V86mzY788hR

The same data elements also have a legacy Age(Malaria) category combination used before the form was revised:

Category COC UID
<5yrs W50DgZrPrUk
>=5years ItGQGDRFD4t

2.2 Exact component-level formulas

Indicator A — l2eIpdluuTI

Uses the default category option combo (DHIS2 sums all disaggregated values server-side):

Role Data Element DE UID Category COC UID
Numerator Persons tested positive for malaria by RDT GEd2F6skCpT (default — all age groups summed) —
Denominator Persons presenting with fever & tested by RDT r6WOvUlcQm6 (default — all age groups summed) —

Indicator B — uiA07P72s2Z

Explicitly names category option combos, mixing old and new age-disaggregation schemes:

Role Data Element DE UID Category COC UID Scheme
Numerator Persons tested positive for malaria by RDT GEd2F6skCpT <5yrs W50DgZrPrUk Old
Numerator Persons tested positive for malaria by RDT GEd2F6skCpT >=5years ItGQGDRFD4t Old
Numerator Persons tested positive for malaria by RDT GEd2F6skCpT ≥5yrs (excl PW) v0QZcUTuyjD New
Numerator Persons tested positive for malaria by RDT GEd2F6skCpT Preg Women (PW) V86mzY788hR New
Numerator Persons tested positive for malaria by RDT GEd2F6skCpT <5yrs (new) Bl7e1tsRGoZ missing from numerator
Denominator ✗ Persons tested positive for malaria by RDT GEd2F6skCpT (default — all age groups) — ERROR: positives added to denominator
Denominator Persons presenting with fever & tested by RDT r6WOvUlcQm6 <5yrs Bl7e1tsRGoZ New
Denominator Persons presenting with fever & tested by RDT r6WOvUlcQm6 ≥5yrs (excl PW) v0QZcUTuyjD New
Denominator Persons presenting with fever & tested by RDT r6WOvUlcQm6 Preg Women (PW) V86mzY788hR New
Denominator Persons presenting with fever & tested by RDT r6WOvUlcQm6 <5yrs + >=5years (old) — missing from denominator

Two errors compound:

  1. The denominator adds RDT positives. #{GEd2F6skCpT} (no COC) in DHIS2 notation means the default aggregation — the sum across all category option combos. Placing it in the denominator means the denominator equals positives + tested. When TPR is 75%, the denominator is inflated by approximately 75%, reducing reported TPR from ~75% to ~75 / (1 + 0.75) ≈ 43%. At higher positivity the distortion is greater.

  2. The numerator omits the new-scheme <5yrs positives (Bl7e1tsRGoZ), so it undercounts positives from facilities reporting under the current NHMIS form version. The denominator uses only new-scheme tested categories, so it cannot incorporate data from facilities that reported under the old age-disaggregation scheme.

Pre-transition: falsely elevated TPR (suppressed by DHIS2). Before Kebbi State facilities switched to the new NH19_Malaria Testing age-disaggregation scheme, new-scheme tested categories for r6WOvUlcQm6 reported zero. Indicator B’s denominator therefore collapsed to #{GEd2F6skCpT} default (old-scheme positives) + 0. With the numerator approximating the same positives total, Indicator B would return TPR ≈ 100%. DHIS2 suppresses or does not return rows where a percentage indicator exceeds 100%, which is why Indicator B returns zero rows for 2020–2021 in the Kebbi data — not because there was no malaria testing, but because the implicit value was implausibly high. The post-transition deflation (~26% from ~76%) and the pre-transition inflation (~100%) are two sides of the same formula errors.

3 Empirical comparison — Kebbi State, 2020–2026

Code 
library(data.table)
library(tsibble)
library(ggplot2)
library(scales)

KEBBI_DIR <- "~/Library/Mobile Documents/com~apple~CloudDocs/Projects/MG2/reports/Nigeria/"

3.1 Facility-level TPR (MG2 method, independent of DHIS2 indicators)

Code 
kebbi <- readRDS(file.path(KEBBI_DIR, "kebbi_cache.rds"))
setDT(kebbi)
cat("Kebbi rows:", format(nrow(kebbi), big.mark = ","),
    "| Date range:",
    format(min(as.Date(kebbi$Month)), "%Y-%m"), "to",
    format(max(as.Date(kebbi$Month)), "%Y-%m"), "\n")
#> Kebbi rows: 1,860,580 | Date range: 2020-01 to 2026-04
Code 
# Age-disaggregation schemes
NUM_OLD <- "Persons tested positive for malaria by RDT_>=5years"
NUM_NEW <- c("Persons tested positive for malaria by RDT_≥5yrs (excl PW)",
             "Persons tested positive for malaria by RDT_Preg Women (PW)")
NUM_U5  <- "Persons tested positive for malaria by RDT_<5yrs"
DEN_OLD <- "Persons presenting with fever & tested by RDT_>=5years"
DEN_NEW <- c("Persons presenting with fever & tested by RDT_≥5yrs (excl PW)",
             "Persons presenting with fever & tested by RDT_Preg Women (PW)")
DEN_U5  <- "Persons presenting with fever & tested by RDT_<5yrs"
NUM_VALS <- c(NUM_U5, NUM_OLD, NUM_NEW)
DEN_VALS <- c(DEN_U5, DEN_OLD, DEN_NEW)

# Facility-level (leaves only)
rdt <- kebbi[effectiveLeaf == TRUE & data %in% c(NUM_VALS, DEN_VALS),
             .(orgUnit, Month, data, original)]
rdt[, component := fifelse(data %in% NUM_VALS, "numerator", "denominator")]

# Sum age groups per facility-month (na.rm = TRUE)
fac_sums <- rdt[, .(
  value = if (all(is.na(original))) NA_real_ else sum(original, na.rm = TRUE)
), by = .(orgUnit, Month, component)]

fac <- dcast(fac_sums, orgUnit + Month ~ component, value.var = "value")

# MG2 validity rules
fac[, num_adj := fifelse(
  is.na(numerator) & !is.na(denominator) & denominator > 0, 0, numerator
)]
fac[, valid := !is.na(num_adj) & !is.na(denominator) &
               denominator > 0 & num_adj <= denominator]

# Monthly aggregate
mg2_monthly <- fac[valid == TRUE, .(
  mg2_tpr = sum(num_adj) / sum(denominator) * 100,
  mg2_num = sum(num_adj),
  mg2_den = sum(denominator),
  n_fac   = .N
), by = Month]
mg2_monthly[, date := as.Date(Month)]
setorder(mg2_monthly, date)

cat("Monthly rows:", nrow(mg2_monthly), "\n")
#> Monthly rows: 76

3.2 DHIS2 indicators — state-level

Code 
dhis2_cache <- readRDS(file.path(KEBBI_DIR, "kebbi_dhis2_indicators.rds"))
dhis2_correct <- as.data.table(dhis2_cache$correct)
dhis2_errored <- as.data.table(dhis2_cache$errored)

cat("Correct indicator rows:", nrow(dhis2_correct),
    "| Range:", format(min(dhis2_correct$date), "%Y-%m"), "to",
    format(max(dhis2_correct$date), "%Y-%m"), "\n")
#> Correct indicator rows: 76 | Range: 2020-01 to 2026-04
cat("Erroneous indicator rows:", nrow(dhis2_errored),
    "| Range:", format(min(dhis2_errored$date), "%Y-%m"), "to",
    format(max(dhis2_errored$date), "%Y-%m"), "\n")
#> Erroneous indicator rows: 16 | Range: 2022-01 to 2026-04
Code 
# Side-by-side comparison for months where both DHIS2 indicators are present
overlap <- merge(
  dhis2_correct[, .(date, correct_tpr = value, correct_num = numerator, correct_den = denominator)],
  dhis2_errored[, .(date, errored_tpr = value, errored_num = numerator, errored_den = denominator)],
  by = "date"
)
overlap[, tpr_gap_pp := round(correct_tpr - errored_tpr, 1)]
overlap[, den_ratio   := round(errored_den / correct_den, 2)]

overlap[, .(date,
            correct_tpr = round(correct_tpr, 1),
            errored_tpr = round(errored_tpr, 1),
            tpr_gap_pp,
            correct_den = format(correct_den, big.mark = ","),
            errored_den = format(errored_den, big.mark = ","),
            den_ratio)]

Indicator B’s denominator is approximately 1.8–2.8× larger than Indicator A’s denominator for the same months. The TPR gap is 45–50 percentage points: Indicator A shows ~74–77% positivity; Indicator B shows ~25–30%.

3.3 Three-way comparison plot

Code 
mg2_line   <- mg2_monthly[, .(date, value = mg2_tpr,   source = "MG2 (facility-level, independent)")]
corr_line  <- dhis2_correct[, .(date, value,           source = "DHIS2 Indicator A (l2eIpdluuTI)")]
err_line   <- dhis2_errored[, .(date, value,           source = "DHIS2 Indicator B (uiA07P72s2Z)")]

all_series <- rbindlist(list(mg2_line, corr_line, err_line))

ggplot(all_series, aes(x = date, y = value, colour = source)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 0.8) +
  geom_point(size = 1.5, alpha = 0.8) +
  scale_colour_manual(values = c(
    "MG2 (facility-level, independent)" = "#1f78b4",
    "DHIS2 Indicator A (l2eIpdluuTI)"   = "#33a02c",
    "DHIS2 Indicator B (uiA07P72s2Z)"   = "#e31a1c"
  )) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = function(x) paste0(x, "%"), limits = c(0, NA)) +
  scale_x_date(date_breaks = "1 year", date_labels = "%Y") +
  labs(
    title    = "Kebbi State — RDT test positivity rate: two DHIS2 indicators vs. facility-level",
    subtitle = "Indicator B (red) adds RDT positives to denominator (~50pp gap).\nIndicator B absent 2020–2021 because pre-transition TPR would exceed 100% (suppressed by DHIS2).",
    x = NULL, y = "TPR (%)", colour = NULL
  ) +
  theme_bw() +
  theme(legend.position = "bottom", legend.text = element_text(size = 8))

3.4 Numerator and denominator decomposition

The divergence in denominator values confirms the formula error:

Code 
numden <- merge(
  mg2_monthly[, .(date, mg2_num, mg2_den)],
  dhis2_correct[, .(date, d2c_num = numerator, d2c_den = denominator)],
  by = "date", all = FALSE
)
numden <- merge(
  numden,
  dhis2_errored[, .(date, d2e_num = numerator, d2e_den = denominator)],
  by = "date", all.x = TRUE
)
setorder(numden, date)

numden[!is.na(d2e_den), .(
  date,
  `MG2 num`          = format(mg2_num, big.mark = ","),
  `Ind A num`        = format(d2c_num, big.mark = ","),
  `Ind B num`        = format(d2e_num, big.mark = ","),
  `MG2 den`          = format(mg2_den, big.mark = ","),
  `Ind A den`        = format(d2c_den, big.mark = ","),
  `Ind B den`        = format(d2e_den, big.mark = ","),
  `Ind B den / Ind A den` = round(d2e_den / d2c_den, 2)
)]

Indicator B’s denominator is consistently 1.8–2.8× Indicator A’s denominator for the same months. The ratio tracks TPR: when positivity is high, adding all positives to the denominator inflates it proportionally more.

3.5 Why MG2 counts differ from DHIS2 Indicator A

The raw facility-level sums (before validity filtering) match DHIS2 Indicator A (l2eIpdluuTI) exactly every month. All differences between the MG2 TPR series and Indicator A come from MG2’s facility-level validity rules, which DHIS2’s pre-computed indicator does not apply.

Code 
total_fm    <- nrow(fac)
missing_den <- fac[is.na(denominator) | denominator == 0, .N]
num_gt_den  <- fac[!is.na(numerator) & !is.na(denominator) &
                     denominator > 0 & numerator > denominator, .N]
missing_num <- fac[is.na(numerator) & !is.na(denominator) & denominator > 0, .N]

cat("=== Facility-month validity breakdown ===\n")
#> === Facility-month validity breakdown ===
cat(sprintf("Total facility-months:                %6d\n", total_fm))
#> Total facility-months:                 49081
cat(sprintf("Missing/zero denominator (excluded):  %6d  (%4.1f%%)\n",
            missing_den,  missing_den  / total_fm * 100))
#> Missing/zero denominator (excluded):     632  ( 1.3%)
cat(sprintf("Numerator > denominator (excluded):   %6d  (%4.1f%%)\n",
            num_gt_den,   num_gt_den   / total_fm * 100))
#> Numerator > denominator (excluded):      522  ( 1.1%)
cat(sprintf("Missing numerator, valid den (→ 0):   %6d  (%4.1f%%)\n",
            missing_num,  missing_num  / total_fm * 100))
#> Missing numerator, valid den (→ 0):      188  ( 0.4%)
Code 
monthly_raw  <- fac[, .(raw_num  = sum(numerator, na.rm = TRUE),
                        raw_den  = sum(denominator, na.rm = TRUE)), by = Month]
monthly_filt <- fac[valid == TRUE, .(filt_num = sum(num_adj),
                                     filt_den = sum(denominator)), by = Month]

monthly_raw[,  date := as.Date(Month)]
monthly_filt[, date := as.Date(Month)]

diff_tbl <- merge(monthly_raw[, .(date, raw_num, raw_den)],
                  monthly_filt[, .(date, filt_num, filt_den)],
                  by = "date")
diff_tbl <- merge(diff_tbl,
                  dhis2_correct[, .(date, d2_num = numerator, d2_den = denominator)],
                  by = "date")
diff_tbl[, `:=`(num_diff = raw_num - filt_num, den_diff = raw_den - filt_den)]

cat("=== Raw sums match DHIS2 Indicator A exactly? ===\n")
#> === Raw sums match DHIS2 Indicator A exactly? ===
cat("Months where raw_num != d2_num:", diff_tbl[raw_num != d2_num, .N], "\n")
#> Months where raw_num != d2_num: 0
cat("Months where raw_den != d2_den:", diff_tbl[raw_den != d2_den, .N], "\n\n")
#> Months where raw_den != d2_den: 0

cat("=== Months where MG2 filtered != raw (differs from DHIS2) ===\n")
#> === Months where MG2 filtered != raw (differs from DHIS2) ===
diff_tbl[num_diff != 0 | den_diff != 0,
         .(date,
           `DHIS2 A (=raw) num` = format(d2_num,   big.mark = ","),
           `MG2 filt num`       = format(filt_num,  big.mark = ","),
           `num removed`        = format(num_diff,  big.mark = ","),
           `DHIS2 A (=raw) den` = format(d2_den,   big.mark = ","),
           `MG2 filt den`       = format(filt_den,  big.mark = ","),
           `den removed`        = format(den_diff,  big.mark = ","))]

The raw facility sums equal DHIS2 Indicator A to the digit. Every difference comes from three validity rules applied at facility level:

  1. Numerator > denominator — primary driver. Facility-months where recorded positives exceed tested count are dropped. Because the numerator was inflated above the denominator, removing them reduces the numerator more than the denominator.

  2. Missing/zero denominator. Facility-months with no tested count are excluded entirely. DHIS2 sums the numerator for those rows regardless.

  3. Missing numerator → 0 (minor, offsetting). When a facility has a valid tested count but no positives recorded, MG2 imputes zero. The effect (0.4% of facility-months) is smaller than the exclusions above.

The magnitude of removed counts has grown in recent years (2024–2025: up to ~9,000 positives removed in a single month), suggesting a deteriorating data quality signal worth monitoring independently of the TPR calculation itself.

4 Programme context: who uses which indicator?

Nigeria’s NHMIS/DHIS2 is the official source of malaria surveillance data for the National Malaria Elimination Programme (NMEP), the US President’s Malaria Initiative (PMI), and all major implementing partners. Test positivity rate is a core programme monitoring metric.

Key users who cite Nigeria DHIS2 TPR:

Organisation Programme States / scope Source
NMEP / Federal Ministry of Health National Malaria Strategic Plan 2021–2025 National NMEP 2021
USAID / PMI Nigeria Malaria Operational Plan FY 2023 11 focus states incl. Kebbi USAID 2023
MSH / PMI for States (PMI-S) HMIS quality improvement, Kebbi ~80%, Zamfara 62–89% Kebbi, Zamfara, Ebonyi Data for Impact 2023
Malaria Consortium Seasonal malaria chemoprevention (SMC) monitoring Kebbi, Sokoto, Bauchi GiveWell 2023

No programme report or operational plan names the specific DHIS2 indicator UID or formula. All sources describe TPR generically as “positive tests / total tests performed among febrile patients.” Indicator B (uiA07P72s2Z) carries exactly the “TPR” acronym that programme staff and dashboard users would search for; Indicator A uses different terminology.

5 Published studies that cite Nigeria DHIS2 TPR without specifying the indicator

A consistent gap in the recent literature on Nigeria malaria is that authors extract test positivity data from DHIS2 without documenting which of the two indicators (or which underlying data elements) was used:

  • Dahunsi et al. (2025) extracted RDT TPR for Oyo State from DHIS2, 2021–2022 (TPR 70–82%). Defines TPR as “positive tests as a proportion of total tests performed among febrile patients.” No DHIS2 indicator name or UID cited. Funded by PMI; implemented by MSH. Malaria Journal 24:65. PMC12016084

  • Oladoyin et al. (2025) selected study facilities in Sokoto and Oyo on the basis of “the highest TPR reported on the DHIS2.” Defines TPR as “number of positive tests divided by total tests performed.” No indicator name or UID given. PMI Insights / BMGF-funded. Malaria Journal 24 (2025). PMC12595662

  • Gnansounou et al. (2025) extracted “number of positive RDT results and total RDTs administered” from DHIS2 across Benin, Côte d’Ivoire, Nigeria, and Uganda. No indicator name or UID specified for any country. PMI-funded. Malaria Journal 24 (2025). PMC12219610

  • Data for Impact / D4I (2023) reported Kebbi TPR “relatively stable at around 80%” and Zamfara 62–89% from DHIS2, as part of an HMIS data quality evaluation. No indicator name cited. USAID-funded. Nigeria DHIS2 Evaluation Baseline Results. D4I 2023

  • Multi-country concordance study (2025) used DHIS2 aggregate TPR as a facility selection criterion across Nigeria, Uganda, Benin, and Côte d’Ivoire. Neither defined nor attributed to a specific indicator. PMC12866365

In all five cases, if Indicator B (uiA07P72s2Z) was used (via dashboard selection or an analytics API call referencing its UID), the reported TPR would be deflated by roughly 50 percentage points — and the direction and magnitude of any intervention effect, state comparison, or trend analysis would be affected accordingly.

For the studies selecting facilities based on “high TPR on DHIS2”: if Indicator B was the indicator consulted, facilities with genuinely high positivity would show TPR ~25–30% rather than ~75–80%. The ranking of facilities by positivity is preserved (both indicators use the same underlying counts), but the absolute values reported are systematically wrong.

6 Implications for the DHIS2 Reporting Checklist

This case illustrates items in Domain 1: Data Dictionary of the DHIS2 Reporting Checklist:

1c. Indicator specification: If a pre-computed DHIS2 indicator is used, report the indicator name and UID. Verify the indicator formula against instance metadata (DHIS2 maintenance app or API call to /api/indicators/{uid}.json) before reporting.

The following practice would have detected and prevented this error:

# Retrieve and inspect indicator formula from DHIS2 API
# Replace with your base_url / credentials
url <- paste0(base_url, "api/indicators/uiA07P72s2Z.json",
              "?fields=name,numerator,denominator,numeratorDescription,denominatorDescription")
resp <- httr::GET(url, httr::authenticate(username, password))
httr::content(resp)
# → denominator expression reveals #{GEd2F6skCpT} (positives) added to denominator

Reporting the UID enables readers to: 1. Retrieve the formula from any copy of the instance metadata. 2. Verify it matches the generic definition stated in the Methods section. 3. Detect discrepancies (as demonstrated here) without access to the live DHIS2 instance.

The secondary error — mixing old and new age-disaggregation schemes in a single indicator — illustrates Domain 5: Instance Attributes (documenting form version and category scheme transitions). Nigeria’s NHMIS underwent a form revision that introduced a new three-group age breakdown (<5yrs, ≥5yrs excl PW, Preg Women) to replace the two-group scheme (<5yrs, >=5years). Indicators created against the old scheme are not automatically updated; they continue to reference legacy category option combo UIDs that return no data for facilities that have transitioned to the new form.

6.1 Evidence of the NHMIS form transition

The NHMIS 2013 form (dataset UID lyVV9bPLlVy) was succeeded by the 2019 version (O82o1WlMisO). Both include the two data elements used in these indicators. The 2019 form introduced the NH19_Malaria Testing category combination with three age groups, replacing the two-group Age(Malaria) scheme. In current DHIS2 metadata, GEd2F6skCpT (positives) and r6WOvUlcQm6 (tested) are assigned NH19_Malaria Testing; Age(Malaria) now appears only for community form data elements.

Kebbi State facility data shows a near-simultaneous rollout in January 2021: no facilities reported under the new scheme before that month; by February 2021 the old scheme had zero active facilities. This pattern is consistent with a coordinated national DHIS2 form deployment rather than organic adoption. The transition explains both extremes of Indicator B’s behaviour: before January 2021, the new-scheme tested categories returned zero, collapsing the denominator to positives only and driving TPR to ~100% (suppressed by DHIS2); after February 2021, the formula error (positives added to denominator) produces the observed ~50pp deflation.

An additional scope difference: GEd2F6skCpT (positives) also appears in the Community Monthly Summary Form; r6WOvUlcQm6 (tested) does not. If community health extension worker positives flow into state aggregates without a corresponding tested denominator, Indicator A’s default-aggregation denominator may undercount tested cases from community reporting, deflating TPR further.

6.2 What cannot be answered from available metadata

The available evidence reveals the current formula and its empirical consequences, but several historical questions require additional investigation:

  • When was Indicator B created? The DHIS2 API exposes created and lastUpdated timestamps for indicator objects. If Indicator B predates the NH19 transition (≈ 2020), the old-scheme COC UIDs in its numerator may have been valid at the time — the error arising from configuration drift when data element category assignments changed without the indicator formula being updated.

  • Was Age(Malaria) ever assigned to GEd2F6skCpT or r6WOvUlcQm6? An audit trail of category combination assignments — if the Nigeria DHIS2 instance maintains one — would confirm whether these elements previously used Age(Malaria), explaining Indicator B’s old-scheme COC UIDs. Some DHIS2 versions log metadata changes; others do not.

  • How consistently is the tested denominator recorded? A facility-level comparison of months with valid numerator vs. valid denominator for r6WOvUlcQm6 would reveal whether the “tested” field is systematically missing for certain facility types or ownership categories. Systematic gaps would bias any TPR estimate regardless of indicator choice.

  • Do community records contribute to state aggregates? If GEd2F6skCpT values from the Community form are included in state rollups without a corresponding tested count, both indicators understate the true denominator.

6.3 Building a transparent TPR with MG2

An alternative to relying on pre-built DHIS2 indicators is to compute the ratio directly from facility-level data element values with an explicit, documented formula. The MG2 R package (build_combined_dataset(), operation = "Ratio") provides this capability: users select specific data element × category combinations for numerator and denominator, choose which age-disaggregation scheme applies to their analysis window, apply standard validity rules (missing numerator → 0; numerator > denominator → excluded), and save a fully documented formula alongside the computed dataset.

Section 3 of this report is a worked example. The analysis code specifies every data element name and category included in the numerator and denominator. This is reproducible without access to the live DHIS2 instance, and it documents precisely which age groups are summed and under which data quality rules. None of the five programme reports and published studies cited in Section 5 provided equivalent formula detail. Building TPR from data elements rather than pre-computed indicators makes that documentation an automatic byproduct of the analysis workflow — and makes formula errors like those in Indicator B detectable before results are published or used to guide programme decisions.

7 Summary

Finding Detail
Two DHIS2 indicators in Nigeria HMIS nominally cover the same metric Indicator A (l2eIpdluuTI) and Indicator B (uiA07P72s2Z)
Formula errors in Indicator B Denominator adds total RDT positives; numerator omits new-scheme <5yrs; old-scheme tested not covered
Empirical impact (post-transition, 2022+) Kebbi State: Indicator A 74–77% TPR; Indicator B 25–30%; gap ~48 pp
Empirical impact (pre-transition) Indicator B would return ~100% TPR; DHIS2 suppresses values >100% → Indicator B returns no rows for 2020–2021
Denominator inflation factor Indicator B’s denominator 1.8–2.8× larger than Indicator A’s for the same months
Indicator most likely selected in error Indicator B (uiA07P72s2Z) includes the “TPR” acronym; Indicator A (l2eIpdluuTI) does not
Denominator quality concern (Indicator A) “Persons presenting with fever & tested by RDT” may in practice capture total fever attendees; untested patients inflate denominator → TPR deflated
All bias pathways summarised Indicator A: deflated if untested fever cases in denominator. Indicator B: deflated post-transition (positives in denominator); inflated/suppressed pre-transition
Literature gap 5 published studies / programme reports cite Nigeria DHIS2 TPR (2021–2026) without naming indicator UID or formula
Checklist items triggered Domain 1c (indicator UID + formula verification); Domain 5 (instance form version / category scheme)

Analysis: MG2 R package facility-level computation + DHIS2 analytics API (Kebbi State OU: M689V9w3Gs3). Data: Nigeria national HMIS (DHIS2), January 2020 – April 2026. Nigeria DHIS2 credentials required to reproduce DHIS2 indicator series; cached results embedded.

Analysis run: 2026-05-30 10:01:16.844807
